organization.organization_background: Utkrusht is a proof-of-skills marketplace that helps agile teams find talent with strong fundamental concepts within days. We help aspiring professionals improve their profile with continuous improvement in their skills, documenting their journey and helping them fulfill their potential. We primarily build, conduct, proctor and disseminate proof-of-skill assessments in Technical domains like AI, ML, Fullstack development, High scale distributed systems, DevOps etc but do help non-tech roles like Sales, HR, Marketing etc

role_context: A Software Engineer at the INTERMEDIATE level (3-5 years experience) is expected to recognise when a brute-force or wrong-shape implementation must be replaced with the right algorithmic primitive (graph traversal, trie, heap, dynamic programming) and apply it inside an existing module while pinning the new contract with rigorous correctness AND performance tests. They typically own focused refactors such as replacing a recursive DFS with Dijkstra over a 12K-node graph, swapping a linear scan over 180K product names with a popularity-aware Trie, replacing a full sort over 200K keys with a heap-of-k plus deque-based window expiry, or replacing a wrong greedy with bottom-up DP for regional inputs. They make engineering trade-offs (build cost vs query cost, recursion depth vs iterative BFS, greedy vs DP) and justify them with measurable tests on production-realistic inputs. They keep public function signatures stable when refactoring internals, validate edge cases (same-node, unreachable, empty prefix, three-way matches, expired events, regional denominations), and reason about Big-O in terms of V, E, n, k, prefix_length, and amount. They rely on senior/expert engineers for advanced graph algorithms (Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, max-flow), segment trees, suffix automata, KMP from scratch, bitmask DP, and DP-on-trees. In day-to-day work they own end-to-end algorithmic refactors inside one or two modules and ship them with a performance test that pins the new bound.
questions_prompt:
Please ensure the questions you ask cover the key functional, logical, architectural, implementation, and optimisation areas from the competency scope for {name}, matched to {proficiency} proficiency ({yoe} years experience).

Focus the assessment on these DSA topic areas and sub-skills only:
- Foundational graph algorithms inside a real product module:
  - BFS/DFS on adjacency lists for connectivity and reachability
  - Dijkstra's shortest path with a priority queue under non-negative weights
  - Topological sort and simple cycle detection on DAGs
  - Edge cases: same-node path, unreachable destination, disconnected components
  - Iterative traversals to avoid stack-depth issues on large graphs
- Trees and tries:
  - Trie for prefix-based autocomplete with precomputed top-k at each node
  - Build cost vs query cost trade-offs
  - BST traversals and simple LCA on small trees
- Heaps and priority queues:
  - Top-k via min-heap of size k vs full-sort
  - K-way merge and sliding-window medians via two heaps
  - Scheduling with a priority queue
- Dynamic programming (bounded):
  - 1D bottom-up DP (coin change, LIS, classic knapsack)
  - Small 2D bottom-up DP (edit distance, simple grid problems)
  - Defining state, transition, and base case explicitly
  - Recognising when greedy is provably wrong and DP is required
- Hashing and sliding window:
  - Rolling aggregates with FIFO queue + counts map
  - Expiring entries in the same pass that adds new ones
  - Avoiding duplicate pairs in multi-match groups
- Algorithm-aware refactoring:
  - Replacing O(n^2) inner loops with O(n) hash-grouped passes
  - Replacing full sort with heap-of-k when k << n
  - Replacing recursive DFS with iterative BFS when stack depth is a concern
  - Replacing greedy with DP when greedy is provably wrong
  - Keeping public function signatures stable while changing internals
- Big-O reasoning under realistic data sizes:
  - Justifying complexity in terms of V, E, n, k, prefix_length, amount
  - Picking performance-test inputs that distinguish the new bound from the old
  - Reasoning about amortised vs worst-case bounds
- Tests for INTERMEDIATE DSA code:
  - Correctness tests on hand-built fixtures including the bug regression case
  - Performance tests on production-realistic input sizes that pin the new bound
  - Pinning edge cases
- Quality and idioms:
  - Idiomatic code in the project's primary language
  - Standard library / built-in primitives only (no specialised third-party DSA libs unless justified)

This is intermediate proficiency (3-5 years), so the candidate is expected to make engineering trade-offs and justify them with tests. Let the candidate be able to finish this within 40 minutes.

Prefer tasks that assess ability to recognise the right algorithmic primitive for a real product problem, integrate it cleanly inside an existing module, handle non-trivial edge cases, and pin the new contract with both correctness AND performance tests on realistic input sizes.

The tasks should be realistic, focused, and work-item driven, such as replacing a recursive DFS with Dijkstra inside a routing module, swapping a linear scan for a popularity-aware Trie inside an autocomplete module, replacing a full sort with a heap-of-k plus deque-based window expiry in a trending dashboard, or replacing a wrong greedy with bottom-up DP inside a refunds module.

Avoid expert/research topics: Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, A*, max-flow, segment trees from scratch, suffix automata, KMP from scratch, bitmask DP, DP-on-trees, custom balanced BSTs. Avoid abstract leetcode puzzles, syntax recall, and trick questions. Ask for practical implementation, refactoring, and engineering judgment inside a real product module.

The goal is to evaluate applied competence, engineering judgment about algorithmic trade-offs, and the candidate's ability to ship a refactor with rigorous tests within the expected time constraints.
